intro: Our guiding principles are set in stone.
lead: Open Free Books is free internet textbooks for learners from elementary school through university — static, open source, and built for the web. These are the values we will not trade away as the catalog grows.

principles.title: Free
principles.body: Every book, every chapter, every learner — no paywalls, no premium unlocks, no subscription that gates the syllabus. Free is not a trial; it is the product, forever.

principles.title: Open
principles.body: Source on GitHub, improvements via pull requests, forks for classrooms and regions. Open means you can see how the book was made, fix a typo today, and ship a better edition tomorrow — not a PDF locked behind a login.

principles.title: Curriculum
principles.body: Not a wiki. Wikis excel at scattered facts; they do not carry a learner from prerequisite to mastery. We publish opinionated paths: ordered chapters, assessed skills, and a point of view about what to teach next — structure on purpose, not everything about X.

principles.title: Independent
principles.body: Built for learners and teachers, not for ad networks or investor growth charts. Sustainability comes from community contribution and shared infrastructure, not from turning students into the product.

principles.title: Durable
principles.body: Static HTML in an open repository — readable in a browser today, archivable tomorrow, forkable for decades. Your syllabus should not vanish when a vendor sunsets digital textbooks or changes the terms.

principles.title: Crafted
principles.body: Books are authored HTML with care: figures, interactives, and JSON Canvas where they help understanding — not generated slop or print PDFs pasted on the web. Quality is a deliberate choice on every page.

principles.title: Inspectable
principles.body: Every change has a commit. Teachers can adapt a fork for their syllabus with diffs and review, not opaque exports from a CMS. If something is wrong, you can find who wrote it and fix it.

principles.title: Web-native
principles.body: The medium is the open web: links, search, accessibility, and embeddable demos — not a file you download once and never update. Textbooks should behave like the rest of the internet learners already use.
